front: What does the P vs NP problem ask, and why is it a fundamental limitation of algorithmic power?
back: It asks whether every problem for which a solution can be verified quickly (in polynomial time, NP) can also be solved quickly (in polynomial time, P). If P ≠ NP, it means there is a fundamental limitation: for many important problems (like the Traveling Salesperson Problem), no efficient, exact algorithm exists.

front: What is an undecidable problem? Provide a classic example.
back: An undecidable problem is one for which no algorithm can be constructed that always produces a correct yes-or-no answer. The classic example is the Halting Problem, which asks whether a given program with a given input will eventually halt or run forever. It is provably impossible to solve algorithmically.

An intractable problem is one that can be solved algorithmically, but for which no efficient (polynomial-time) solution is known. The resources (time, space) required to solve them grow so rapidly (e.g., exponentially) that they become infeasible for all but the smallest inputs. Most NP-Complete problems are considered intractable.

front: What does it mean for a problem to be NP-Complete?
back: An NP-Complete problem is one that is both in NP (solutions can be verified quickly) and NP-Hard (at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP). If any NP-Complete problem has a polynomial-time algorithm, then all problems in NP would also be in P. This class defines a boundary of what is considered 'efficiently solvable'.

front: What is the concept of an unsolvable problem? How does it differ from an intractable one?
back: An unsolvable (or undecidable) problem is one for which no algorithm exists *at all* to solve it, regardless of the time or space resources provided (e.g., the Halting Problem). An intractable problem is solvable by an algorithm, but the algorithm requires an impractical amount of resources, making it effectively unsolvable for large inputs.
